data.title: GVAL: An Interesting Alternative For Global Value
data.description:
The Cambria Global Value ETF (GVAL) is a relatively unknown fund that may deserve more attention. The fund has only $582.3 million in assets under management even though it has been trading in the market for over 12 years. Yet, last year, GVAL produced a return of almost 56%, not including dividends. Many international funds certainly enjoyed strong results in 2025, but the approach taken by the Cambria Global Value ETF is somewhat unique in that it selects stocks from both developed and emerging markets.

In this analysis, I will explain why I think the fund may be worth holding in an account that is looking for added exposure to international stocks that may be missed by larger, more traditional passively-managed ETFs. The gains from 2025 certainly should not be considered typical, as international funds trail their US counterparts over longer periods of time. Nonetheless, the 13.5% gain posted by GVAL year-to-date shows that the momentum built from last year’s holdings is not ready to fade just yet.

ETF Overview

The Cambria Global Value ETF follows an interesting strategy to select its portfolio. GVAL, like all Cambria funds, is actively managed. The fund’s fact sheet starts with tales of Benjamin Graham and David Dodd in explaining how their holdings are selected. GVAL selects the top 25% cheapest stock markets from a pool of 45 countries using a cyclically adjusted PE ratio. From there, the fund managers use metrics like price-to-book, price-to-sales, price-to-cash flow, and EV/EBITDA to choose the 10 most undervalued stocks of the top 30 largest companies from each of those countries.

The result is a portfolio that looks cheaper on paper than even the typical foreign value ETF. As of April 24, there were 120 stocks held by GVAL. As of March 31, the most recent update from Cambria, the stocks included in the fund have an average PE ratio of 13.1 and trade almost equal to their book value.

As might be expected for a value-oriented fund, the largest sector allocation for the Cambria Global Value ETF is financials at 29.1%. Materials is second at 13.5%, and utilities and energy stocks are the only other two sectors with a weight of at least 10%. The fund is relatively well diversified, with only the health care sector completely unrepresented by its holdings.

The country allocation shown above is certainly notable considering that each one holds a relatively equal share in the fund. Columbia was at the top of the list as of March 31 at 11.1%. Singapore was second at 9.8%. I have often looked for ETFs that had meaningful exposure to Poland besides the iShares MSCI Poland ETF (EPOL), and GVAL fits that description with a 9.6% weight toward that European market. A report last year from Oxford Economics showed that Poland was the home for 10 of the 20 fastest growing cities in all of Europe. While most of the countries on the list would be considered emerging markets, two developed economies, Austria and Great Britain, are included in the top five. Unlike passive funds that follow a specific index and are often rebalanced on a quarterly schedule, the holdings for GVAL can change at any time during the year.

Normally I would go into more detail about the top 10 holdings for an ETF that I was analyzing, but few of the stocks held by GVAL on the list below would be considered household names. At the top of the chart with a weight of 3.65%, AT & S Austria Technologie & Systemtechnik (ASAAF) is a tech company that produces and distributes electronic components. The stock has already gained almost 200% since January 1 of this year. Second on the list is Moneta Money Bank (GMYYF), a diversified bank based in Prague. The only other holding with an allocation greater than 2% of the fund is Komercni Banka, another Czech-based financial stock. KGHM Polska Miedz (KGHPF), fifth on the top 10 holdings, is a Polish mining company that has seen triple digit gains over the last year as metal prices have risen significantly.

GVAL distributes dividends on a quarterly timetable, but there have been three quarters in the last two years when there were no distributions to be made. The total annual payouts decreased in both 2024 and 2025. The fund currently has a yield of 2.86% over the last 12 months.

Analysis of GVAL

Since its inception in 2014, GVAL has only produced an annualized return of 5.92%. Cambria uses the MSCI ACWI Index as a comparison for the fund’s returns, and that index has gained 10.0% during the same time period. However, it is the recent performance of GVAL that is much more noteworthy.

According to Morningstar, the Cambria Global Value ETF has been one of the very best funds in its category over the last month, one year, three years, and five years. The fund is in the top 10% of returns in each of those periods when compared to over 300 peer funds. Even over the last decade, GVAL has been able to produce double-digit annual returns and finish in the top 20% of similar funds.

In the middle of this strong run, there have been some disappointing single years. In 2020, the fund lost over 8% of its value when the average fund in its category produced a small gain. GVAL also finished in the bottom quartile for returns in 2019 and 2021. I think this simply shows the cyclicality for the holdings in a fund like this, where investors can become enamored with emerging markets for a period of time before casting them aside for just as long. The overall returns from the fund over the last 10 years lend more validity to the stock selection methods employed by the fund managers for this ETF.

The expenses for GVAL are slightly below what would be expected from an actively managed international stock fund but above the median for all ETFs. Cambria charges its investors a management fee of 0.59% and an added custodial expense of 0.07% for a total expense ratio of 0.66%. This is certainly not out of line, especially considering the fund's recent returns. In some of its fund literature, Cambria gives data from Morningstar that says this ETF actually compares quite favorably with other foreign value funds that have expense ratios closer to 1%.

Cambria also makes the case that the stocks held by its Global Value ETF are a compelling investment because of their discount to the MSCI ACWI Growth Index. Of course, the stocks in GVAL should always trade at a discount to a growth portfolio, as that is what makes them value stocks, but the gap in valuation since the start of the COVID-19 pandemic has been wider than it was previously. It is worth noting, however, that the discount experienced over the last several years appears to be trending toward an end as we move through 2026.

Risks to Consider

The attraction of a typical value fund is the reduced risk of volatility and significant losses. The Cambria Global Value ETF may not always display the same advantages because a majority of its current holdings are from emerging economies. Countries like Colombia, Singapore, and Thailand give the fund greater exposure to potential political instability, currency fluctuations, and eroding investor confidence. The impact of the current conflict in the Middle East may be greater in emerging markets than in a more developed country that has more options for trade and energy resources. Trade conflicts and logistical congestion could impact these countries’ ability to export their goods and services, decreasing potential growth in their GDP. The lack of liquidity in some emerging markets may also make trading their stocks more expensive and more challenging when trying to adapt to changing conditions in the local economy.

The active management style that is followed by GVAL is susceptible to greater risk as well. There is no guarantee that future returns will look anything like what occurred in 2025. The fund compares itself to the MSCI ACWI Index, which has over 2500 constituents. The effect of just a few bad selections by GVAL’s fund managers could have a greater impact on the fund’s overall returns. The challenges for actively managed funds have been debated for some time, with most all failing to outperform their benchmarks over longer periods.

The Bottom Line

The Cambria Global Value ETF currently has a Buy rating from Seeking Alpha’s Quant analysis. The fund gets high marks for momentum, which should be no surprise given its recent returns, and for risk. The risk score is impacted by the fund’s diversification outside its top 10 holdings as well as its surprisingly low turnover rate from an actively managed ETF. The expense ratio of 0.66% is one of its biggest detractors.

I am not quite as optimistic as SA’s quant rating simply because of the current volatility in world markets and the heightened risks presented by some of the developing countries that are represented in the fund. It is hard to deny GVAL’s track record over the last five quarters. The fund’s portfolio has shown a great deal of resiliency over the last couple of months.

The Cambria Global Value ETF has a four-star rating from Morningstar for its risk-adjusted returns, which is the same as other, better-known international ETFs like the Schwab International Equity ETF (SCHF) and the Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets Index Fund ETF (VEA). By calling GVAL a hold, I am not intending to be overly pessimistic about its future returns, but I think the added risks of emerging markets here make the fund less attractive for a more conservative investor like myself.

data.title: Covenant Logistics Did Not Share The Improvement In The Market In Q1 2026
data.description:
Covenant Logistics (CVLG) released its Q1 2026 results and provided a very interesting data point to analyze what might be the first quarter of recovery in a long time in the trucking industry.

The company’s results did not show improvement, something that surprised me, given that the industry (as shown, for example, on the CASS index) is already improving.

Management said this was mostly because the winter storms of January and February, plus fuel cost headwinds, offset the rate gains and better volumes that appeared in March and April (already in Q2). We will see if this improves in the coming quarters.

On a longer-term basis, I continue to believe CVLG has not shown great capital allocation and that the stock is already pricing a full recovery in capital returns, and then some more. That does not feel coherent with the cyclical nature of the industry, nor with the company’s historically low returns on assets. For this reason, I maintain a Hold on CVLG.

Q1 2026 results

I was excited to read CVLG’s results because the trucking market has started to show some recovery in rates, as shown by indicators like the Cass index. Shipments are still weak across the board, but rates are climbing, and these are generally the first cyclical indicators to improve, particularly when there is supply tightness.

In CVLG’s case, management pointed to a similar dynamic in the call. The company said the quarter included two of the worst months (January and February, because of the winter storms) and one of the best months it had experienced in the past three years, with positive momentum continuing into April. Management also said it was seeing the effect of declining driver and truck capacity, improving demand in some segments and geographies (emphasis on some only), and a stronger pipeline for committed truckload capacity from its customers in the Dedicated segment, signaling tightness in those pockets of the market.

Unfortunately, the recovery is not yet evident in the company's financials.

Freight revenue increased, mainly because of the acquisition of a managed freight services company in Q4 2025, but adjusted operating income declined, indicating margins remain very pressured.

The main problematic segment was Expedited. This is the company’s more operationally leveraged truckload segment, because it requires high utilization to cover fixed costs. Many of the trucks carry two drivers, meaning high labor costs.

In the quarter, expedited freight revenue fell 10%, with adjusted segment operating income collapsing to breakeven from close to $5 million a year ago. The problem came from lower utilization (miles per tractor) because of the harsh winter, the company said.

This dynamic is unfortunately the opposite of what one would want to see in an early-cycle recovery. The company did get a rate improvement, but utilization was too low, particularly in January and February. We will need to see if in Q2 they can maintain rates and grow utilization.

Dedicated looked better, but this is basically optical because of an easy comp. Dedicated freight revenue increased 11%, and operating income tripled to ~$4.5 million. The problem is that last year’s comparison was depressed by avian influenza in the company’s poultry fleet, one of its most important verticals, so the improvement does not necessarily indicate a normal cyclical inflection in returns.

An indication of what management believes about the cycle is that the company continues to sell capacity by selling more trucks than it buys. Average tractor age increased to 26 months from 20 months a year ago.

This might indicate that management is still wary that this is not a complete recovery, or also that the company needs to delever and therefore is 'forced' to sell assets at a cyclical bottom. Net indebtedness fell by ~$50 million in the quarter. If this were the case, it would be super problematic because a cyclical company has to invest during downcycles and divest during upcycles. This is the contrary, a bad sign for future returns across the cycle (important for valuation as we will see below).

The asset-light businesses were also mixed. Managed freight (mainly brokerage) revenue increased 60% YoY, but this came mostly from an acquisition in Q4 2025. Operating income was flat YoY, meaning margins deteriorated. This deterioration came from the impact of higher rates, which generally hurt brokerages when they are fast. Warehousing revenue increased 15% organically, helped by a new customer, but adjusted operating income fell too because it takes time to ramp up profitability in new revenues, according to management.

Overall, the company believes the cycle will improve throughout the year. Management said rate improvements negotiated in Q1 2026 should start to show up in Q2 2026, because new bids take a few weeks to take effect. It also said April was better than March and that Q2 should improve over Q1, with Q3 improving again over Q2.

A related key question is how much of this rate strength will be eaten by other costs. Driver pay is already a topic because part of the upcycle is driven by driver supply tightness (management believes DOT enforcement of driver rules, mainly for foreign drivers entering the US, has already removed 2% to 3% of capacity from the market). Management said drivers are starting to get tight for the first time in about 40 months and that driver pay could rise by mid-single digits, or high single digits if the market gets really hot. It also said driver pay is roughly 30% of the total cost and therefore could absorb 30% to 40% of the first round of rate increases before other inflation items. On top of this, we have costs like new equipment, which management also recognized as more expensive in the call.

On the downside, I agree with management that the main risk is macro. The Iran conflict has already affected fuel and supply-chain conditions, and a prolonged shock could pressure the economy before trucking demand has really recovered. Fuel itself is usually recovered via fuel surcharges, but the second-order effects on freight demand, consumer spending, and supply chains can wreck the supply-driven recovery via a demand-driven crunch.

Valuation

There are two ways to analyze CVLG, or other trucking companies for that matter.

One is speculative, as a vehicle to express a view on the cycle. I have no special view on that. If investors want to play a tightening trucking market, CVLG can work for some time, especially if Expedited finally shows the operating leverage management expects. If the cycle returns to previous peaks, the company will make a lot more money.

The problem is that this speculation becomes a “greater fool” game once the price is already above long-term fair value, and CVLG is already well beyond past ATHs, even from the post-pandemic boom.

From a longer-term perspective, we have to think about returns on capital across the cycle. Last time I analyzed the company, I estimated that CVLG could generate about $55 million of net income across the cycle, based on historical industry cycles, the company’s own returns, and the contribution from TEL (a leasing subsidiary not consolidated in the company's financials).

Today, the company already trades at a market cap of $860 million (25.3 million shares at $34), or roughly 15x that cyclical-average figure.

That does not seem attractive for a capital-intensive, cyclical, relatively commoditized trucking business in a cycle that has not yet fully turned, and with the company needing to cut capacity into the recovery because of leverage.

I do not think the Q1 2026 results change the long-term thesis, although they help flesh out a speculative view on the cycle in trucking. The problem is that the stock price already seems quite ahead of that development.

From all of this, I continue to consider CVLG a Hold.

data.title: Pinnacle Financial Partners: Post-Merger Goals Are On Track
data.description:
Shares of Pinnacle Financial Partners (PNFP) have been a mixed performer so far in 2026 as the company has focused on integrating Synovus, after completing what was essentially a merger of equals. I last covered Pinnacle in January, rating the stock a “buy,” given my view the deal should be significantly accretive. Since then, shares have lost about 5% as investors contemplate potential risk to banks from challenges in private credit. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit Pinnacle, determine how the Synovus integration is proceeding, and whether shares are poised for better performance going forward.

In the company’s first quarter, Pinnacle Financial Partners earned $2.39, which beat estimates by $0.07. I would note that all year-over-year results are dramatically skewed by the inclusion of Synovus in its financials. During the quarter, the company also realized $275 million of merger costs as it integrates systems, optimizes headcount, and so forth. This spending is in line with my expectations, and it should gradually diminish, with the company still on track for a complete conversion within 12 months from now. This spending is excluded from its adjusted earnings.

Looking first at its balance sheet, core deposits were up $1.9 billion sequentially, an 8% annualized pace, and total deposits were up 6% to $100 billion. The company’s deposit performance was very impressive to me, especially considering it remains a fairly competitive environment for deposits. Given the strength in customer deposits, Pinnacle was able to let higher-cost brokered deposits roll off, and this total now stands at $8.6 billion, down $500 million sequentially. These deposits are competed on solely through rates, which tends to make them costlier.

Loans grew by $2.1 billion sequentially to $85 billion on an organic basis, or up 10%. Within its portfolio, growth was focused on business lending, in particular asset-backed finance. Pinnacle primarily focuses on business lending with $48 billion of C&I loans, followed by $24 billion of commercial real estate, and $13 billion of consumer exposure (primarily mortgages and home equity loans). Pinnacle has less than a third of its portfolio in CRE, a fairly low exposure, which I view positively. While that sector may have passed the worst of its pressures, challenges remain.

Within loans, exposure to non-depository financial institutions (“NDFI”) has become a major focus point of investors as it is the primary channel through which banks have exposure to private credit. PNFP ended the quarter with $7.3 billion of NDFI loans, which is about 9% of its portfolio. In general, I view sub-10% exposure as low in the industry, though the nature of this lending can vary significantly. In particular, I view mortgage warehousing as being exceptionally low risk. It has $1.7 billion of private credit exposure within business intermediation, including $339 million to BDCs. It also has $1.4 billion in other private credit loans. Its exposure is exclusively at the top of the capital structure, with each special purpose vehicle over-collateralized and ring-fenced with significant haircuts to borrowing bases that are monitored monthly. None of its private credit exposure is nonperforming as of Q1, speaking to the conservatism of its vehicles, and I am comfortable with its exposure, though it remains an area to monitor.

During the quarter, it increased its securities portfolio by $750 million, and it now stands at 19.8%. The portfolio has a 4.2% yield, so I see limited benefit from roll-off and expect portfolio yield to be fairly static over the coming quarter. This portfolio is very high quality, with 99% qualifying as high-quality liquid assets (“HQLA”). With a 3-year average duration, movements in portfolio yield will be very gradual, even if market rates shift dramatically. This portfolio also provides a liquidity buffer, which is why PNFP is positioned to grow loans a bit more quickly than deposits over time, especially after its capital ratio increases (more on that below).

Net interest income (“NII”) was $933 million, and its net interest margin (“NIM”) expanded to 3.53%, towards the high end of its 3.45-3.55% guidance. NIM is structurally higher post-merger because bonds and loans held by Synovus are remarked to current yield levels. With interest rates higher today than many of these fixed rate assets, this process lifts yields and NIM post-acquisition, though it is a day 1 capital headwind. Accretion was a 17 bp benefit, and this NIM is non-cash. Beyond this, the integration of SNV and its low-cost liabilities has created an 8bp structural uplift to NIM. I expect the accretion benefit to slowly narrow over time, and NIM should remain within a couple bps of current levels.

Turning to credit quality, net charge-offs remain modest at 23 bps. Allowances cover 1.19% of loans, which is up 1 bp sequentially as it slightly downgraded its macroeconomic outlook. It has $459 million of nonperforming loans, or about 54 bps of total loans. Allowance for credit losses is ~2.2x NPLs, which is within the 2-2.25x range I like to see, especially given the secured nature of its loans. Plus, upon acquiring SNV, it had to mark down loans based on their creditworthiness, similar to the process described above for interest rates, and there was a ~$483 million mark. This should further insulate the business from credit risk.

Pinnacle maintains a reasonable balance sheet with a tier 1 common equity capital ratio (CET1) of 9.8%. While this is a sufficient capital level, it is towards the low end of what I would like to see. Given its organic growth targets, PNFP should be operating in the 10-10.5% range. As you can see below, capital is down about 0.9% from last year. This is primarily due to the merger. As noted above, when a bank acquires another, its assets are marked to market, crystallizing unrealized losses from a CET1 perspective.

Whereas many regional banks have higher CET1 ratios but significant unrealized losses within AOCI on their securities portfolio, most of PNFP’s assets are now marked within the CET1 ratio, so its published ratio is a better indicator of its financial health. Include AOCI, its CET1 ratio would only drop 20 bps to 9.6%. As the company retains a portion of earnings this year, I expect CET1 to end between 10 and 10.3%, depending on the exact speed and nature of its loan growth. Once Pinnacle gets through most of its merger costs, that could open the door to meaningful buybacks, though organic growth will be the priority. Shares offer a 2% dividend yield.

Alongside results, management reaffirmed full-year guidance, which targets fairly ambitious balance sheet growth. It aims for 9-11% loan growth and 8-10% deposit growth. Its NIM should remain around 3.5%. The company delivered on this growth rate during Q1 and simply needs to maintain it through year end. I have remained a bit cautious that loan demand could sputter if the Iran war’s impact on energy prices is worse than consensus, but with a solid Q1 and strong pipeline, PNFP appears well positioned to achieve this growth.

Back in January, I was targeting $9.90-$10.50 in 2026 EPS, excluding any one-time M&A costs. Even without any revenue synergies (of which I remain skeptical), I saw the company earning over $11 next year. I continue to view both of these outlooks as being on track. Shares are trading less than 10x, which I believe is attractive given underlying growth and pending cost synergies. While headline CET1 is a bit low (likely a key driver of its lower valuation), that reflects M&A accounting, and its adjusted capital is consistent with many peers. Plus, I expect capital to be at target by year-end, even with substantial loan growth. As that occurs, it should enable multiple expansions and for shares to push past $110. I remain a buy.

data.title: Vaalco Energy: The Growth Story Is Gaining Momentum
data.description:
Vaalco Energy (EGY) is entering the first leg of its production growth story. The company is in the process of bringing its FPSO vessel and Cote d'Ivoire field back online, resulting in the return of sizable production volumes. Further, the company is implementing its first drilling campaign in several years in offshore Gabon. Initial well production rates have been very strong.

The ramp in production will allow sufficient cash flow to permit the company to fund the second and third legs of its long-term vision to meaningfully grow production. Additionally, the stock trades at just 2.5x 2027 estimated EBITDA, assuming a realized oil price of $65/barrel.

This allows EGY to remain extremely attractive despite generating a 60% total return since my Buy rating in June 2025. I am upgrading the stock to a Strong Buy on the back of strong execution and a fundamental shift in oil markets.

Recent Developments

EGY’s stock weakened significantly in 2025 as roughly 15% of its production was taken offline due to the refurbishment of the Cote d'Ivoire FPSO. That vessel has now completed its refurbishment and has returned to the Baobab Field. Hookups are in progress with a return to production estimated to occur in Q2.

This field was originally producing over 4,000 bbls/d, making it a sizable add on to EGY’s existing production. As production ramps back to pre-refurbishment levels, EGY will begin expansion efforts in the later parts of 2026.

This field will be developed by commencing drilling Phase 5 to tie in five additional wells to the FPSO. This should allow FPSO production to double in the first half of 2027.

This alone should allow the company to report meaningful growth in both production and revenues in the coming year. Layering in the impacts of the recent Gabon drilling campaign will take production growth a step further.

On April 21st, the company reported significant production values from a well recently brought online in the Etame field (Gabon). The 14H well was placed in a 325-meter-thick reservoir with excellent geological properties.

As a result, the well initially flowed at 4,850 barrels per day. This well will net the company 2,850 barrels per day after accounting for its 58% working interest in the project. If the results of this well are repeatable by any measure, investors are likely to see production gains that may exceed the company’s 2026 estimates.

Near And Long Term Outlook

In my opinion, the production estimates provided by EGY are light. The company is forecasting for flat y-o-y production despite the return of the FPSO. This production is likely to offset the production lost via the sale of the company’s Canadian assets earlier this year.

The return of the FPSO will cause production to be back loaded into the second half of 2026. This leaves Egypt and Gabon to do the heavy lifting for the company.

In Egypt, the company reported achieving 11,000 BOE/d rates in Q1, giving the company a solid base to operate from. Although growth in this region was solid in 2025, EGY appears to be structuring this field for maintenance activity levels in 2026. Capital investment will be less than half of that deployed in 2025 as the company focuses on workovers and recompletion activities.

Overall, capital investment by EGY is getting a ramp up in 2026. The company plans to have a capital budget of approximately $325m, about $90m more than in 2025. This capital will be largely deployed to complete FPSO tie-ins as well as kicking off the Phase 5 drilling campaign in Cote d'Ivoire.

Additionally, roughly one-third of the capital budget will be deployed to the Phase 3 drilling program in Gabon. The recent success in this field has the potential to be significant for the company. If a comparable second well is drilled, the company may plan to accelerate investment in this area. The development plan of this field included five optional wells that could get exercised if favorable results are obtained.

If the drilling program yields results that are anywhere near comparable to the 14H well, Gabon volumes could easily blow past full year projections. Current full year production levels are projected to reach a modest 8,750 barrels per day. This would put the field up just 1,000 bpd from Q1 levels. Even a few modest performing wells should easily allow the company to exceed this target.

Looking out longer term, I see even more value. As management continues to methodically pursue its growth strategy, 2027 stands to be a step change in production. The company stated in the Q4 conference call that it expects to exit 2026 at a production rate of 25,500 bpd.

Achieving this threshold would drive production 25% higher than YE 2025. The additional scale will also improve the company’s OPEX per barrel. This figure has suffered recently due to the Cote d'Ivoire asset being offline. Investors should expect this metric to improve significantly in 2027.

While initial data may indicate that current guidance is conservative, it also paints a picture of severe undervaluation for the company. This production level and an assumed realized price of just $65.50/barrel allow the company to generate nearly $600m in estimated 2027 revenue. Using 2024 OPEX levels (the last time the FPSO was in service), I estimate that the company is capable of generating $300m in annual EBITDA.

Under these assumptions, the current share price values the company at just 2 times 2027 EBITDA. This metric expands to 2.5x if estimated EBITDA is conservatively lowered to $250m. In either scenario, a valuation range of 2-2.5x indicates that EGY’s potential is simply not being seen by the market.

Free Cash Flow Outlook

If you examine the production forecast provided above, you’ll note that the company has ambitious plans that stretch out into the next decade. Funding the capital required for these projects is usually where the rubber meets the road for companies the size of EGY.

2026 cash flows are moderately challenged by the high capital spend planned for the year. At an assumed $65/barrel realized price, I model EGY as producing a cash outflow of $100m. The company currently has $59m in cash on the balance sheet, so it appears likely the company will slip into a slight net debt position.

However, the duration of the current situation in the middle east can alter how the balance sheet is positioned going forward. Since the beginning of early March, crude oil prices have averaged above $90/barrel. This uplift will help to minimize the company’s cash outflows.

Around 65% of EGY’s production is currently unhedged, leaving sizable exposure to current pricing. While I don’t expect this conflict to last long enough to allow EGY to emerge as cash positive in 2026, every little bit helps to keep the balance sheet in as strong of a position as possible.

Regardless, EGY expects heavy spending in Q1 as work on the FPSO completes. The $100m capital budget for the quarter is likely to result in a temporary draw on the company’s revolver. That balance should alleviate some as spending moderates and production increases in the second half of the year.

Risks

Despite ambitious growth plans, EGY remains a small producer. With operations producing just 20,000 barrels per day, any operational mishaps can be quite impactful. For example, when the FPSO was taken offline in early 2025, OPEX per barrel rose by roughly 15%.

This sensitivity to operational performance combined with the need for repeatable offshore success can make EGY a bit higher on the risk spectrum than your traditional onshore producer.

Investor Key Takeaways

  1. EGY is sitting at an upward inflection point in production levels. The company will be bringing online its Cote d'Ivoire FPSO in short order.
  2. The company is also reporting strong well results in offshore Gabon with more wells expected to be drilled this year. If the program encounters similar success to its most recent well, that program may double to 10 wells.
  3. The company’s Egyptian assets are profitable with many of the cash receivable issues behind them. This asset serves as a baseload cash flow engine while the company focuses on the immediate needs of its offshore business.
  4. The company is ramping up capital spending in 2026 as it advances the first leg of its growth plans. I expect the company to be cash flow negative this year due to the high investment rate.
  5. The conflict in the Middle East has the potential to offset some of this cash outflow. The balance sheet should remain strong with a healthy cash reserve to draw on as capital investment is heavily 1st half weighted.
  6. Longer term, EGY has several legs to its growth strategy. Equatorial Guinea and expansion of the Cote d'Ivoire FPSO are development projects for the later parts of this decade. In total, the company plans to grow production by 3x by 2030.
  7. I believe EGY trades at just two times 2027 EBITDA, making the company worthy of a Strong Buy recommendation.

data.title: Broadcom: Driving Growth In A Competitive Market
data.description:
I upheld my buy recommendation for Broadcom in my last article on the stock, citing the likelihood of considerable revenue growth from its artificial intelligence ("AI") business. I am maintaining my buy recommendation in this article, as Broadcom’s revenue visibility has strengthened significantly since my last article, driven by the company adding three additional AI chip customers and management providing information on how much some of them plan to spend.

Additionally, the company keeps bumping up its AI revenue estimates. The company's original projection for AI revenue growth in fiscal year ("FY") 2026 was 60%. Management now believes FY 2026 AI revenue will accelerate well beyond 60%. Chief Executive Officer (”CEO”) Hock Tan’s commentary on the earnings call has also given investors greater confidence in the management’s AI growth projections for 2027, which the market previously viewed as speculative.

Although the stock still has some valuation risk, management’s projections for continued AI growth, the broadening of its AI chip users to non-hyperscaler customers, and its rapidly expanding networking business are reasons for me to keep recommending Broadcom as a buy for growth investors who expect AI infrastructure spending to keep rising over the next several years.

New Customers

By the time I wrote my last article, the market had already confirmed that Broadcom's first and second customers were Google (GOOGL)(GOOG) and Meta Platforms (META), and had speculated that the third was TikTok's parent company, ByteDance (BDNCE).

CEO Hock Tan identified Anthropic (ANTHRO) as the recipient of a $10 billion order during the fourth-quarter 2025 earnings call (which some call the fourth customer) and said the following about that customer on the first-quarter FY 2026 call:

For Anthropic, we are off to a very good start in 2026 for 1 gigawatt of TPU...For '27, this demand is expected to surge in excess of 3 gigawatts of compute. Our XPU franchise, I should add, [goes] beyond TPUs.

A February 26, 2026 news article indicates that Fujitsu (FJTSY) is already receiving new AI chips from Broadcom, and last, an OpenAI (OPENAI) press release in October 2025 stated:

OpenAI and Broadcom today announced a collaboration for 10 gigawatts of custom AI accelerators. OpenAI will design the accelerators and systems, which will be developed and deployed in partnership with Broadcom.

Some call OpenAI the sixth customer, even though Broadcom has not officially numbered its customers. Although the partnership with OpenAI was announced in 2025, the first time CEO Tan mentioned OpenAI on an earnings call was on the first quarter FY 2026 earnings call: "OpenAI [is] deploying in volume their first-generation XPU in 2027 at over 1 gigawatt of compute capacity." He also said: "We have line of sight to achieve AI revenue from chips, just chips, in excess of $100 billion in 2027."

I view Hock Tan's commentary on the first-quarter call about its six XPU customers as a massive "de-risking" event for the stock, as it strengthened revenue visibility into 2027. Analysts have significantly raised their 2027 revenue estimates in late March, likely after fully assessing the company's AI chip revenue projections; Wall Street raised its 2027 annual revenue estimates from $134.53 billion on February 23, 2026, to $156.16 billion on March 28, 2026, and later bumped it up to $158.47 billion on April 20.

The market has already begun reacting to the upward 2027 revenue revisions and greater visibility into AI revenue. Since the beginning of April, the stock has risen over 27%, as of April 17.

Still, for investors buying today to get a good return on the current stock price (As of the April 17 close), the company will need to do more than just rapidly grow AI chip revenue, because the market is concerned that its rapidly growing chip business could erode margins and reduce earnings per share ("EPS") growth.

Alleviating Market Concerns Over Margins

Since the company reported fourth-quarter 2025 results, there is evidence that AI chips have lower gross margins than the software business, weighing on the stock price. The faster AI chip revenue grows, the more the mix shift erodes the higher-margin advantage Broadcom gained from its acquisition of VMware, which is one reason the stock dropped 11% after the company released fourth-quarter earnings, despite beating analysts' estimates.

CEO Hock Tan said on the fourth quarter 2025 earnings call: "The AI revenue has a lower gross margin than our -- obviously, the rest of our business, including software, of course." Broadcom's CEO later had an interesting response to an analyst's question about concerns over lower AI chip gross margins and whether that would spill over to lower operating margins:

As we do more and more AI revenue to be so much that we get the operating leverage on our operating spending that operating margin will deliver dollars that are still high level of growth from what it has been. So we expect operating leverage to benefit us at the operating margin level, even as gross margin will start to deteriorate, high level.

In other words, yes, gross margins will deteriorate as AI chip revenue rises, but what some market players are missing is that operating leverage will more than offset gross‑margin dilution, and operating income and EPS will actually grow faster.

Additionally, AI chips often need to be connected together to be useful. As companies network together more and more AI training and inferencing XPU (AI chip) clusters to handle larger and larger models, the opportunity for Broadcom to sell more of its AI networking products increases. Remember, as the number of AI chips in a data center increases, the scale and amount of the networking equipment required to connect them grow faster.

As AI models become increasingly complex, hyperscalers are adopting Broadcom's most advanced switches, such as the Tomahawk 6, the world's first 102.4 Terabits/sec (Tbps) switch, and 1.6-terabit DSPs (Digital Signal Processors), which are part of an optical module, to manage the extreme traffic flows between AI accelerators (XPUs and GPUs).

CEO Hock Tan said on the first quarter FY 2026 earnings call:

The Tomahawk 6 that we introduced over 6 months ago -- or in fact, closer to 9 months ago, we're the only one out there. And our customers and the hyperscalers wants to run with the best networking and with the most bandwidth out there for their clusters. So we are seeing huge demand for this only 100 terabit per second switch out there. So that's driving a lot of demand. And couple that with running bandwidth on scaling out optical transceivers at 1.6 terabit. We are again the only player out there doing DSP at 1.6 terabit. That combination is driving, I would say, the growth of our networking components even faster than our XPUs are growing, which is already pretty remarkable.

Since networking equipment has a higher margin, the company could offset lower-margin chip sales with vastly increased sales of networking equipment. The company's first-quarter FY 2026 AI networking revenue increased 60% over the previous year's first quarter and accounted for 33% of total AI revenue. CEO Tan said on the earnings call, "In Q2, we project AI networking to accelerate a lot more and grow to 40% of total AI revenue."

If you are a growth investor, increased operating leverage from higher AI chip sales and higher-margin networking equipment sales is one reason to invest in this stock, despite the bad optics of lower gross margins from rising AI chip sales.

What Investors Should Look For Moving Forward

One big issue for Broadcom and other chip manufacturers is that leading-edge 2nm (nanometers) and 3nm wafers, High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM), and advanced CoWoS (Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrates) have become a serious bottleneck for delivering AI chips to customers on time.

On the first quarter FY 2026 earnings call, CEO Tan confidently said:

Beyond technology, we provide multiyear supply agreements as our customers scale-up deployment of their compute infrastructure. Our ability to assure supply in these times of constrained capacity in leading-edge wafers, in high-bandwidth memory and substrates ensures the durability of our partnerships, and we have fully secured capacity of these components for '26 through '28.

Investors should monitor management's commentary during future earnings calls or investor conferences on whether it can maintain a strong supply chain for these items to ensure the on-time delivery of AI chips to customers.

Next, Broadcom's Custom AI Accelerator (XPU) Program has become one of the company's key drivers of revenue growth. For the company to reach goals such as $100 billion in AI chip revenue in 2027, it must continue to diversify its customer base and complete the deployment of its existing orders, measured in gigawatts ("GW") of deployed compute, not units shipped. Up until now, management has mostly discussed what it expects to deliver. Investors should pay close attention in future earnings calls to management's commentary about actual deliveries of AI chips to customers.

Management expects to deliver its first gigawatt of compute to Meta (MTIA) in 2027, which the company believes is a $12–$15B of revenue opportunity. Next is Google. CEO Tan said on the first quarter 2026 earnings call about the hyperscaler:

For Google, we continue our trajectory of growth in '26 with strong demand for the seventh-generation Ironwood TPU. In 2027 and beyond, we expect to see even stronger demand from next generations of TPU.

Also, remember I mentioned earlier in the article that the company expects to ramp Anthropic from 1 GW in 2026 to over 3 GW in 2027 and expects to deliver its first GW deployment to OpenAI in 2027.

Investors should also keep an eye on whether Broadcom finds additional XPU customers. Each new XPU customer has the potential to generate 3–5 years of recurring silicon revenue, giving Broadcom additional revenue visibility. It also dilutes the Broadcom XPU program's concentration in Google (TPU program), Meta (MTIA), and other hyperscalers. As the company's revenue concentration decreases, Broadcom's revenue becomes less volatile, supporting higher valuation multiples. Sustained XPU growth should also boost the company's operating leverage over time.

Lastly, investors should monitor Broadcom's ambitions to position VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) as the AI infrastructure operating system for enterprises. When Hock Tan's company initially purchased VMware, some were initially confused about why a hardware company wanted to purchase a virtualization and cloud computing asset. Well, it turns out that Broadcom's management is very forward-thinking. Enterprises don’t want to be locked into the public cloud or a single GPU or networking vendor, and VMware has the opportunity to become the operating system for on‑prem AI clusters or hybrid clouds (a combination of private and public cloud). Even so, some worried that AI would eventually hurt Broadcom's VMware ambitions. However, CEO Tan said the following on the first-quarter FY 2026 earnings call: "We are confident that the growth in generative and agentic AI will create the need for more VMware, not less."

He also said on the same call:

VMware Cloud Foundation, VCF, is the essential software layer in data centers integrating CPUs, GPUs, storage and networking into a common high-performance private cloud environment. As the permanent abstraction layer between AI software and physical chips, silicon, VCF cannot be disintermediated or replaced. It allows enterprises, in fact, to scale complex generative AI workloads effectively with agility that hardware alone cannot provide.

Once Broadcom gains a VCF customer, it tends to retain them because VMware's software has a significant switching-cost moat. Once enterprises build their private‑cloud stack and become reliant on running workloads on VCF, removing it becomes a multi‑year, high‑risk project.

VCF provides the company with high-margin recurring revenue that aids in offsetting lower-margin XPU revenue. The faster VMware revenue grows, the more the market is willing to raise Broadcom’s multiple. VMware displayed first quarter FY 2026 bookings of more than $9.2 billion, and its annual recurring revenue ("ARR") increased 19% year-over-year. Investors should closely monitor VCF bookings and ARR moving forward.

Balance Sheet

Broadcom's cash and equivalents, according to its first-quarter FY 2026 earnings release, were $14.174 billion; long-term debt was $63.805 billion, plus short-term debt was $2.252 billion, which matches YCharts' figures for long-term debt. Be aware that different financial aggregators include different figures in their calculations for cash and long-term debt. So, you may see different figures listed on financial websites for cash and long-term debt.

Broadcom's debt-to-equity ratio is 0.827, meaning the company conservatively finances its operations with equity rather than debt.

The company has a current ratio of 1.902, indicating sufficient short-term liquidity to fulfill its debt obligations due within a year.

Broadcom's net debt ($51.883 billion) to TTM adjusted EBITDA ($46.049 billion) is 1.13, a low leverage ratio for a large, stable, cash‑generative company like Broadcom, indicating the company is well positioned to pay down its long-term debt.

The company's TTM cash from operations was $29.68 billion, and TTM FCF was $28.91 billion. Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Kirsten Spears said on the first quarter FY 2026 earnings call:

We spent $250 million on capital expenditures...Turning to capital allocation. In Q1, we paid stockholders $3.1 billion of cash dividends based on a quarterly common stock cash dividend of $0.65 per share. During the quarter, we repurchased $7.8 billion or approximately 23 million shares of common stock. In total, in Q1, we returned $10.9 billion to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases...We are announcing our Board of Directors has authorized an additional $10 billion for our share repurchase program effective through the end of calendar year 2026.

S&P Global rates all debt instruments A-, which is an upper-medium investment grade.

Valuation

Broadcom's trailing 12-month price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 28.97, well above its three-, five-, seven-, and ten-year medians, which some may interpret as a sign of overvaluation. An alternative explanation is that the market has priced in increased revenue growth or increased profitability.

The following compares Broadcom's two-year forward P/S/G to its peers and TTM profitability.

Broadcom's two-year forward P/S ratio is 12.15, above the peer median of 7.48 and above its closest comparison, NVIDIA (a company with similar high revenue growth, high gross margin, high FCF margin, and heavy reliance on AI revenue), which has a forward P/S of 10.13, denoting possible overvaluation. However, some may consider it undervalued, given that its P/S/G (0.23) is lower than the peer median (0.51) and NVIDIA's P/S/G (0.33). Given its profitability, FCF margin, and ROIC are above the peer median, some may believe Broadcom deserves a premium P/S/G. Therefore, some may believe the stock trades at fair value or is slightly undervalued compared to its peers. However, since NVIDIA has a better gross margin, operating margin, and ROIC, some may believe NVIDIA's premium P/S/G valuation relative to Broadcom is well deserved and consider Broadcom fairly valued to slightly overvalued compared to NVIDIA.

Broadcom's TTM price-to-earnings (P/E) is 79.23, well above the information technology sector median of 34.16, which some may interpret as overvaluation. An alternative explanation is that the market has priced in significant earnings-per-share ("EPS") growth.

Broadcom has a 2026 forward P/E to analysts' EPS growth ("PEG") ratio of 0.52, calculated by dividing its 2026 forward P/E of 35.58 by its estimated EPS growth of 67.53%. Typically, when a stock trades at a PEG ratio below 1.0, the market considers it undervalued. Investors generally consider a PEG ratio of 1.0 as a sign of fair valuation.

Suppose it traded at a PEG ratio of 1.0; the stock price would be $771.87, around a 90% increase over its $406.54 closing price on April 17.

I am going to compare Broadcom's Enterprise Value ("EV") to EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization) with several other AI chip and networking companies. Still, be aware that this valuation approach doesn't work as well in an EV/EBITDA comparison for non-capital-intensive, software-heavy companies and serial acquirers like Broadcom, and it can skew its valuations to appear more overvalued relative to capital-intensive hardware companies with a smaller software component, like Intel (INTC). As a result, some may consider this comparison less useful for determining relative value than a P/S/G comparison.

The company's Enterprise Value ("EV") to EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization) is 27.51.

Analysts estimate that the company will grow EBITDA by 37.3% to $134.49 over the next two years.

Company Name Forward EV/EBITDA Two-year EBITDA growth Forward EV/EBITDA/growth TTM EBITDA Margin
Advanced Micro Devices 38.87 58.39% 0.67 19.34%
Arista Networks 36.19 21.34% 1.70 43.63%
Marvell 31.77 38.14% 0.83 32.08%
Broadcom 27.71 37.3% 0.74 54.39%
NVIDIA 19.35 25.54% 0.76 61.70%
Qualcomm 9.65 1.74% 5.54 30.68%
Median 31.77 25.54% 0.83 32.08%

Broadcom's forward EV/EBITDA is 27.71, below the peer median of 31.77, which some may interpret as an indication of undervaluation. The company's forward EV/EBITDA/growth of 0.74 is below the peer median of 0.83, another indication of undervaluation, especially since Broadcom's TTM EBITDA margin was 54.39%, well above the peer median, suggesting it deserves a premium forward EV/EBITDA/growth valuation.

Broadcom has a price-to-FCF of 68.42, well above its three-, five-, seven-, and ten-year medians, suggesting overvaluation. However, some may interpret price-to-FCF trading above its medians as the market pricing in rapid FCF growth.

The following is a 10-year reverse DCF using a 10.5% discount rate, based on an 10.5% Weighted Average Cost of Capital ("WACC"), indicating slightly above-moderate risk due to the cyclicality of AI infrastructure. I use a 3% terminal growth rate based on the U.S. long-term inflation rate. I am using the standard calculation for the company's annual FCF of $28.91 billion.

Reverse DCF

Broadcom's stock price of $406.54 is justified if it can grow FCF by 24.7% annually over the next ten years, which is extremely aggressive for a company of Broadcom's size ($1.92 trillion market cap), implying a 9.1× increase in free cash flow over a decade, meaning it would reach around $263.081 billion in FCF in 2036. Not even NVIDIA, one of the fastest-growing megacaps in history, has sustained 25% FCF growth for a decade.

If AI infrastructure keeps growing and VMware maintains high margins, Broadcom could realistically achieve free cash flow growth of 15% to 20% per year over the next decade. With 15% annual growth, the stock price would be $201.55, which is a 50% drop from its April 17 closing price of $406.54. At 20% annual growth, the price would be $290.13, a 29% decline. This leads some to think the stock is overvalued. Still, the market may focus on Broadcom’s AI revenue growth and VMware’s strong margins, which might justify a higher valuation over the next two to three years rather than valuing 15% to 20% annual FCF growth over the next decade.

Risks

Risk: Valuation and Execution

The stock's TTM P/S and TTM price-to-FCF ratios have priced in significant revenue and FCF growth. If Broadcom has any setbacks in its growth story and fails to meet the expectations built into the stock price, the market may sell off the stock. For instance, if Broadcom traded at its three-year price-to-FCF median, its price would be $245.28, a decline of around 40% from its April 17 closing price of $406.54. If the stock traded at its three-year P/S median, its price would be $230.70, a 43% decline from its April 17 closing price.

Risk: Hyperscalers Could Reduce AI CapEx

Although CEO Hock Tan is very confident in Broadcom's growth, there are doubts in the market about the sustainability and profitability of Broadcom’s AI growth. On the company's first-quarter FY 2026 earnings call, Jefferies analyst Blayne Curtis noted that Broadcom's AI revenue grew roughly twice as fast as the overall increase in Cloud Capital Expenditure (CapEx). Curtis also said:

I think given the outlook that you have for '27, you should be a share gainer. I'm just kind of curious your perspective in terms of the pessimism that investors kind of think of that the hyperscalers need to get a return on investment in this year or next year or if not, the year after. I'm just kind of curious your perspective, how you factor that into your outlook.

Investors have had concerns for the last year or two that hyperscalers (Google, Meta, etc.) need to see a clear financial return on their massive AI investments soon. If that return doesn't materialize, the hyperscalers may reduce their AI CapEx, which could interfere with Broadcom's plan to reach $100 billion in AI revenue by 2027. If that happened, investors would likely punish the stock.

However, CEO Hock Tan pushes back against the implication that the company is entirely reliant on hyperscaler CapEx spending. As I explained in my last article on Broadcom, CEO Tan believes that the market has split into two broad categories; one group is the hyperscalers, or "creators," which are the companies that design and train Large Language Models (LLMs). The second group is the enterprise market, which is interested in using AI for inference to achieve productivity gains.

Up until now, the AI infrastructure market has been dominated by hyperscalers training new AI models, with NVIDIA's AI chips leading the LLM training market. However, the AI market is shifting to inferencing, which is the AI monetization phase. Broadcom plans to become the dominant provider of AI chips to companies building platforms for enterprise AI tools, code assistants (chatbots), agentic AI, and consumer AI subscriptions.

Although Broadcom is heavily reliant on only a few customers for inferencing AI chips, including hyperscalers like Google and Meta, the company is increasingly gaining business with customers like Anthropic and OpenAI, and possibly in the future with large enterprises building private AI platforms and Sovereign AI customers (governments). CEO Tan said on the first quarter FY 2026 earnings call, "With respect to [platform builders for enterprise AI tools, code assistants, agentic AI, and consumer AI subscriptions], we are seeing stronger and stronger demand for compute capacity..." In other words, there is a future in which hyperscalers reduce CapEx spending, and the company's other customers may take up the slack, and its AI chip revenue might not collapse entirely.

However, despite Hock Tan's current optimistic view of the company's end markets, it remains heavily reliant on hyperscalers today, and the enterprise inference market remains aspirational and may not be able to fill the gap if hyperscaler revenue declines. Even if AI chip revenue doesn't meaningfully decline if hyperscalers cut CapEx, it could adversely affect investors' view of Broadcom's AI chip business. The market may sell off all stocks associated with AI if hyperscalers pull back on AI spending.

Risk: Customers Building Their Own Chips

Another risk an analyst raised on the earnings call was Broadcom's customers building their own chips internally. JPMorgan Chase analyst Harlan Sur commented on the earnings call:

Hock, there's been a lot of noise around CSPs [cloud service providers] and hyperscalers embarking on their own internal XPU, TPU design efforts, right? We call it COT, or customer-owned tooling...Now on AI, some of these COT initiatives are coming to the market now, but it looks like they're at least 2x less performant than your current generation solutions, 2x less complex in terms of chip design complexity, packaging complexity, IP.

Harlan Sur then asked the following question:

Do you see these COT science projects taking any meaningful TPU, XPU share from Broadcom? And then maybe the second quick question for either you or Charlie is, given that Broadcom's TPU, XPU programs from a performance complexity IP perspective are 12 to 18 months ahead of any of these COT programs, how does the Broadcom team widen this gap further?

CEO Hock Tan answered the question by saying that customers may not have the technical knowledge to build chips as advanced as Broadcom's. Specifically, Hock Tan said:

[A] COT model...[faces] tremendous challenges. One is technology, which is technology as it relates to creating the silicon chips and particularly in XPUs that they need to do the computing and that is needed to optimize and run the -- train and inference on the workloads they produce out their LLM...You need the best silicon design team around. You need cutting edge, really cutting-edge SerDes, very advanced packaging and most -- and just as much, you need to understand how to network clusters of them together. We've been doing this for 20 years, more than 20 years in silicon. And in this particular space today in generative AI, if you're trying to, as an LLM player, to do your own chip, you cannot afford to have a chip that is just good enough. You need the best chips that is around because you're competing against other LLM players. And most of all, you're also competing against NVIDIA, who is by no means letting down their guard.

Despite Hock Tan downplaying the risk, the possibility of another company successfully implementing COT remains valid. CEO Tan even admits that while COT competition may still be a way off, it will happen eventually. So, while the risk may not be short-term, investors must monitor the COT risk in the long term.

Broadcom Is Still A Buy

Broadcom trades at a valuation that inflates its execution risk. Investors who emphasize valuation or are risk-averse should stay away from this stock. However, although some valuation measures, such as the P/S and P/FCF ratios, flash overvaluation, analysts estimate strong operating leverage and forecast high EPS growth: 2026 EPS growth of 67.53% and 2027 EPS growth of 58.57%. The market may not have priced in that EPS growth yet. Broadcom has a 2026 forward PEG ratio of 0.52. The stock has an implied 90% upside over the next year from its $406.54 closing price on April 17 if the market eventually prices in a fair-value PEG ratio of 1.0.

Broadcom is one of the most dominant AI infrastructure providers for the central nervous system of AI data centers. One analyst (CounterPoint) forecast that Broadcom will have only 60% of the ASIC market in 2027. I have seen other analysts estimate that it has captured as much as 75% of that market in 2025. An estimated 60%-75% market share in the custom AI chip market puts Broadcom in an excellent position to capture growth in the AI inference market.

The company also has a dominant position in the networking market, with an estimated market share of over 60% in Ethernet switching. Broadcom's networking products solve an emerging bottleneck in AI: networking speed. As AI clusters grow, the most important factor has shifted from chip speed to networking speed. Since Broadcom has some of the most advanced products in the networking space, such as Tomahawk 6, Jericho 4, and its Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) product, Bailly, the growth of high-speed data centers is a strong secular tailwind that directly expands its networking revenue opportunities.

Investors also shouldn't underestimate the importance of VMware's acquisition, a high-margin software business that can partially offset potential downside from the hardware industry's volatility.

If you are a growth investor who believes that AI infrastructure represents a strong investment opportunity over the next two to three years, Broadcom could be a wise investment.
